region_of_origin: East Asia (China, Japan, Korea); widely cultivated in Southeast Asia and Mediterranean regions

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium persimmon (approximately 168 g) is a typical single serving, providing about 111 kcal and 25 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Persimmon, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron taken directly from HPB Foodo record for persimmon (raw). Additional micronutrient values (potassium, vitamin C, saturated fat, cholesterol) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09193 (Persimmons, Japanese, raw) where HPB does not report the field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 for persimmon is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, which lists persimmon (Diospyros kaki) at GI ~50 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 25.2 g) / 100 ≈ 12.5 for a 168 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100 g) with meaningful potassium (161 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Persimmon is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure regulation, and it should be consumed as whole fruit rather than juice to preserve fibre and avoid concentrated sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~50) with 15 g/100 g carbohydrate; dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) and tannins partially attenuate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may include persimmon in controlled portions (1 medium fruit, ~168 g) as part of a balanced meal plan, monitoring total carbohydrate intake and avoiding dried persimmon which contains concentrated sugars exceeding 50 g/100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.02 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and rich polyphenol content (tannins, beta-carotene, vitamin C) with anti-oxidative properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Persimmon is cardioprotective-friendly due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and high antioxidant polyphenol content; regular consumption as whole fruit may support endothelial health and reduce oxidative LDL modification.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in antioxidants (vitamin C 7.5 mg/100 g, beta-carotene, proanthocyanidin tannins) with anti-inflammatory polyphenol profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persimmon may be included in an anti-inflammatory diet for rheumatoid arthritis given its antioxidant and polyphenol content; consume ripe fruit to avoid high astringent tannin levels in unripe fruit that may cause gastrointestinal discomfort.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Persimmon (Diospyros kaki) is a sweet, orange-coloured fruit native to East Asia, widely cultivated in China, Japan, Korea, and increasingly in Southeast Asia and Mediterranean countries. The fruit has a smooth, thin edible skin and soft, jelly-like flesh when fully ripe, with a honey-sweet flavour and subtle astringency from tannins that diminish as the fruit ripens. Two main commercial varieties exist: the astringent Hachiya type, which must be fully soft before eating, and the non-astringent Fuyu type, which can be eaten while still firm. Per 100 g, persimmon provides approximately 66 kcal, 15 g carbohydrate, 0.7 g protein, and 0.4 g fat, with 2 g dietary fibre and 8.8 g natural sugars. It is a notable source of vitamin A (81 mcg RAE), vitamin C (7.5 mg), vitamin E (0.73 mg), and potassium (161 mg), and contains proanthocyanidin tannins and beta-carotene with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Its glycemic index is approximately 50 (low-moderate), making it suitable for most people including those managing blood glucose when consumed in controlled portions. Persimmon is very low in sodium (8 mg/100 g) and saturated fat, making it appropriate for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. It is consumed fresh as a snack, in fruit salads, desserts, and traditional East Asian preparations including dried persimmon (hoshigaki in Japanese). In Singapore and Southeast Asia it is commonly available in supermarkets and wet markets, particularly during the Chinese New Year season.
common_user_queries: Can I eat persimmon if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of persimmon?
How many calories in a persimmon?
Is Sharon fruit the same as persimmon?
Is persimmon good for heart health?
Can I eat persimmon on a low-sodium diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match, confidence 0.95 per input record). Additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09193 (Persimmons, Japanese, raw), which is an approved secondary source. GI value of 50 corroborated by University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication.
notes: Saturated fat (0.02 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.08 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.19 g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g), cholesterol_mg (0.0 mg), potassium_mg (161 mg), vitamin_c_mg (7.5 mg), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (81 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg (0.0 mcg), vitamin_e_mg (0.73 mg), phosphorus_mg (17 mg), magnesium_mg (9 mg), zinc_mg (0.11 mg), selenium_mcg (0.6 mcg), and water_g (80.3 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09193 (Persimmons, Japanese, raw) as HPB Foodo does not report these fields for this item. Vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg) and vitamin D (0.0 mcg) are confirmed zero for this plant-based fruit per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is 0.0 g confirmed per USDA SR Legacy — persimmon is a whole plant food with no trans fat. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg confirmed — plant food. The rheumatoid arthritis entry in the input JSON contained internally contradictory reasoning (the reason stated anti-inflammatory properties while the suitable field was false and the cautions contradicted the reason); this has been corrected to suitable=true with concern_level=low reflecting the antioxidant and polyphenol profile. GI of 50 classified as low (≤55 threshold). Glycemic load per serving = (50 × 25.2) / 100 = 12.6, rounded to 12.5 for a 168 g medium persimmon serving. No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all fields set to -1 in the template have been populated from USDA SR Legacy or confirmed as 0.0 for plant-based food.
